title: Futures and forwards — locking in the future
subtitle: Futures and forwards eliminate price uncertainty for both buyer and seller. The obligation to transact is what distinguishes them from options.

body.content: A futures contract is a standardized, exchange-traded agreement to buy or sell a specific quantity of an asset at a predetermined price on a future date. A forward contract is economically identical but privately negotiated between two parties — not traded on an exchange. Both create an obligation, not a right. Unlike options, you cannot simply let a futures contract expire worthlessly.

body.label: How futures work mechanically
body.content: Futures are marked to market daily. If you hold a futures contract and the price moves against you, your account is debited that day's loss. If it moves in your favor, your account is credited. This daily settlement — rather than waiting for expiration — is what prevents massive credit risk from accumulating in the system. It also means losses can compound quickly for undercapitalized participants.

items: Commodity producers and consumers: a gold miner locks in a selling price to protect revenue. A jewelry manufacturer locks in a buying price to manage input costs.
Currency management: a company with future foreign currency receivables locks in the exchange rate today.
Equity index futures: institutional investors use index futures to rapidly adjust portfolio exposure — buying or selling the equivalent of millions in stocks without touching individual securities.
Interest rate futures: banks and bond funds use these to hedge duration risk in their portfolios.

body.content: For most individual investors, futures are not a primary tool. Their leverage, daily settlement mechanics, and contract complexity make them more appropriate for institutional use and for investors with specific hedging needs. Understanding how they work is essential for financial literacy — using them requires a separate skillset.

Futures and forwards solve a real economic problem: they allow both sides of a future transaction to lock in price certainty today. The obligation to transact is what gives them this power — and what makes them a serious instrument that demands serious risk management.
